issue_title: cluster connection details
issue_body:
Hi ,
I am testing vespa which is running in cloud , Can you share where i can pass the cluster connection details.
root_cause: Missing configuration mechanism to specify remote cluster endpoint for non-parametric ANN algorithm

issue_title: Is it possible to run ann-benchmark offline?
issue_body: Thanks for your apply
root_cause: User unclear on local installation steps for offline ANN-Benchmarks execution

issue_title: analysing the output after plot is generated for the databases
issue_body:
Hi all,

I was working with benchmarking Milvus and qdrant . i am able to generate the plots for both the db.
please let me know how can we analyse the graph and conclude their performance comparison ?

1. how can we analyse the following plots ? please help me with this.
2. also which plot should we go through in order to conclude the performance ?

![fashion-mnist-784-euclidean_10_euclidean](https://github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks/assets/52161957/42867bd9-eba5-4d33-a678-745bfaaef518)
![glove-100-angular_10_angular](https://github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks/assets/52161957/30e79a9a-8dd2-41e7-b213-bdd1592dab99)


Thanks in advance.
root_cause: Missing documentation on chart interpretation prevents performance analysis of benchmark results

issue_title: qdrant  disabled in the benchmark
issue_body:
Hi all,

i ran the benchmark test and found out that qdrant folder is not even created in the result directory. 
i found out that qdrant was disabled in its configuration file . other than that config file, do we need to enable it anywhere else ?

Thanks in advance.
root_cause: Qdrant algorithm disabled in configuration prevents benchmark execution

issue_title: benchmark stopped with exit code 0
issue_body:
@maumueller @erikbern  
Hi all,
while i was running the benchmark from 10days approx, it stopped with an exit code 0.
1. can you tell me if its the completion of benchmark performance run or there could be some issue ?
2. also, how can we analyse the results after graph plotting has been done ?

![remmina_10 168 1 45:1_10 168 1 45:1_2024325-62740 858156](https://github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks/assets/52161957/d1494443-e36a-4687-a650-19c7031662a0)

Thanks in advance .

issue_title: Consistently prewarming database
issue_body: pg_embedding client currently does pre-warming of database while the same is not happening in other databases, including pgvector. Does this effect the comparision in terms of QPS? And should this be standardized across the board?
root_cause: Inconsistent database prewarming across SUT implementations affects benchmark fairness

issue_title: time taken for benchmarking to finish the execution
issue_body:
Hi all,

i wanted to test the benchmark performance of Qdrant and Milvus and so i ran the run.py script. but it has been 5 days approx and the benchmark is still running. 
please let me know how much more time it might take for the benchmarking to finish ?

Also, please let me know if there is any way to minimize the timing by restricting the dataset or specifying the only 2 db , qdrant and milvus for the benchmark execution.

Thanks in advance.
root_cause: Exhaustive hyperparameter search causes excessively long benchmark execution time

issue_title: db running before running the benchmark
issue_body:
HI @erikbern ,
do we need to run the databases seperately before following the steps in the doc for running the benchmark? i mean the steps mentioned in this repo are only responsible for running the benchmark or it runs the databases also ?
root_cause: Container/service orchestration setup required before benchmark execution

issue_title: @erikbern
issue_body:
@erikbern 
ok. currently i am running the test using,
`sudo python3 run.py --dataset glove-100-angular`
any approximation if you can mention about the number of days it might take ?

_Originally posted by @dipshirajput in https://github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks/issues/493#issuecomment-1993701274_

issue_title: @erikbern
issue_body:
@erikbern 
i ran the run.py day before yesterday. it has reached upto the logs as shown in screenshot.
please give some clarity that how much time it might take more to complete the execution ? and how can we see/analyse which DB is performing better ?
![Screenshot from 2024-03-07 10-32-58](https://github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks/assets/52161957/6f680800-eb4d-4057-9156-da2ab909b3d3)


Thanks in advance.

_Originally posted by @dipshirajput in https://github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks/issues/492#issuecomment-1982359858_

issue_title: time consumed by run.py
issue_body:
Hi all,

please let me know how much time it takes for run.py code to finish the execution ?

Thanks.

issue_title: Running benchmark with custom dataset
issue_body:
Hello,
Currently the repo has pre-defined dataset (glove, SIFT, NYTIMES).
Can I use my own custom dataset(*.csv) instead of the above and run the benchmark ?

Thanks in advance.
root_cause: Custom dataset loader missing from benchmark preparation harness

issue_title: pykgraph unavailable for kgraph
issue_body:
Hi all,

I am not able to install kgraph algorithm using this command -

`sudo python3 install.py --algorithm kgraph
`
I am unable to find pykgraph.

root_cause: Missing pykgraph dependency prevents kgraph algorithm installation via pip

issue_title: Wrong result in using FASHION-Mnist
root_cause: Approximate nearest neighbor algorithm returns non-exact results vs ground truth

issue_title: looking for the results data
issue_body:
I'm developing my own implementation of a knn search algorithm, and  I'm trying to compare with the other implementations . 
However, my hardware is very limited so I'm having a hard time trying to replicate your results. I was wondering if I could get the results data (the once are stored in /results) so I can make the comparison. If so, I appreciate if you could provide a link to it.
Thanks in advance

issue_title: i need some help about connect my database,thanks!!
issue_body:
I have a question. I want to ask someone who can answer me. I want to deploy the database locally on Linux and use ann-benchmarks to test my self-developed vector database. But I don’t know how to connect ann-benmarks to my relational vector database. , where should I configure it in the python code? Maybe I should write some code to connect, right?
I'm new to databases, so my question may seem a bit silly, but I'm hoping someone can shed some light on my confusion, thank you!
root_cause: User unable to load and configure local vector database model into ANN-Benchmarks framework

issue_title: How the sparse datasets are stored?
issue_body:
Hi professor @maumueller ,

As you mentioned the `Kosarak and MovieLens-10M` are sparse dataset and they are packed like a scipy csr format,

So, when I use this distance function in https://github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks/blob/main/ann_benchmarks/distance.py#L104 
 to get train and test objects which are basically list object, right? np.ndarray.

Then I found that len of each row is not the same, so it still stored in compact style, right?

## I guess that does each element in the rows represents the index of the non-zero element in the orignal sparse vector?

```
f = h5py.File(h5_file, 'r')
train, test = dataset_transform(f)
print(type(train))
print(len(train))
for i in train[0]:
      print(str(i), end=' ')
print()
```

issue_title: Query About Calculation of Recall!
issue_body:
Hey ANN Benchmarking Team!

I am deep inside the code and am trying to understand the metrics, recall to be precise. 

Can some one explain me what are dataset_distances, run_distances, count, threshold and epsilon in the below function. 


> def get_recall_values(dataset_distances, run_distances, count, threshold, epsilon=1e-3):
>     recalls = np.zeros(len(run_distances))
>     for i in range(len(run_distances)):
>         t = threshold(dataset_distances[i], count, epsilon)
>         actual = 0
>         for d in run_distances[i][:count]:
>             if d <= t:
>                 actual += 1
>         recalls[i] = actual
>     return (np.mean(recalls) / float(count), np.std(recalls) / float(count), recalls)

    
    
I have some inherent understanding that we are comparing the approximate neighbours with the actual neighbours at a particular threshold. It would be great if someone could explain in plain English how exactly we calculate the recall.

issue_title: New Benchmarking Dataset Enquiry
issue_body:
Hey @erikbern and ANN Benchmarking team!

Thanks for creating and maintaining a clean and sophisticated repository for the community.

I am Anubhav, an NLP Researcher at Advanced Symbolics Inc. Me and my company work in the space of probabilistic sampling of social media data and mining people's opinions out of it (https://www.advancedsymbolics.com). 

I recently went through all the 13 benchmarking datasets included in the repository and discovered that although the datasets belong to a variety of categories (Computer Vision, Recommender System, Movie Ratings etc), there was a lack of datasets from the NLP Domain (only GloVe). GloVe embeddings have been pretty old and there are better and much more sophisticated textual embeddings present in the industry now.

I would like to ask you if you would allow me to contribute a benchmarking dataset of tweets ranging from 1M - 10M. These tweets would be probabilistically sampled and would represent an actual world text/NLP dataset. The test set would contain a set of "n" queries which would be search engine queries from users (for eg. "Top SUVs under $50K ?"). We would then use the latest language models to convert all of the data to embeddings. Finally, we can run the experiments for all the indices on this dataset as well.

**Why should we consider adding this dataset?**
There has been a recent boom of LLMs and vector databases, and many people refer to Ann-benchmarks for it. We also referred to ANN benchmarks to decide which index to choose. But sadly there was no up to date NLP use case included in the repo. 

Please let me know If this plan sounds good to you!

Thanks and Regards,
Anubhav Chhabra
root_cause: Missing NLP benchmark dataset limits evaluation scope for text embedding systems

issue_title: Include number of vectors in the db for benchmarking
issue_body:
Hi @erikbern, We recently benchmarked the performance of two popular open-source databases: Qdrant and Postgres (Pgvector) using the ann-benchmarks repo. It became quite popular on [Twitter](https://twitter.com/NirantK/status/1674110063286571008?s=20). We think the number of vectors can in the DB is also an important factor when comparing performance. For example, have a look at our [results](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1t2-tXID2LJCXdLv1JTPQaYhmMs6woOnK7W7nkEuDsUc)

I did the benchmark against  100k, 200k, ..., and 1M vectors in the DB but had to split the 1M dataset into separate datasets to make it work with the repo (See [this](https://github.com/KShivendu/ann-benchmarks/blob/openai-experiments/ann_benchmarks/datasets.py#L619)) and then I created a [script](https://github.com/KShivendu/ann-benchmarks/blob/openai-experiments/experiment.py#L25) to iterate through them and run them one by one. 

I feel these changes are a bit hacky and we can make them fit more naturally with the code so that it's reproducible and easier to work with. I'd love to hear your thoughts if you think there's a cleaner way to implement this.  Happy to raise a PR for the final design that we agree upon :)

Thanks!

issue_title: Throughput (exposed as Queries per second (1/s)) needs to be measured and not derived from latency (1.0 / attrs["best_search_time"])
issue_body:
In theory, if a system has a latency of 1 millisecond for a request, one might think the system can handle 1000 (1/0.001) requests per second, implying a direct correlation between latency and throughput (considering the throughput as the inverse of latency). However, in real-world scenarios, this correlation does not hold due to several reasons:

 - **Concurrency**: Many systems can process multiple requests simultaneously. This means that while the system may take 1ms to respond to a single request, it can also process hundreds or thousands of other requests within that same 1ms timeframe, assuming sufficient resources are available. We can argue that this is not much of a concern in this type of single client benchmarks but please read the points bellow.

 - **Resource Saturation**: A system may experience an increase in latency as the load increases due to the saturation of system resources. This could be due to CPU limits, memory, network bandwidth, or I/O capacity, among others. The system's throughput would level off and could even decrease under extreme load conditions, even while latency continues to rise. If we just focus on the best search time like we're doing now we're not really measuring the implications of a long running benchmark on the resulting throughput. 

- **Queuing Delays**: In many systems, requests are queued before processing. Even if a single request can be processed quickly, if many requests are lined up for processing, the overall latency will be higher. This can happen even if the system's throughput is high.

 - **Network and System Behavior**: Latency can also be affected by network factors like propagation delays, transmission medium, routing, etc., or system-level factors like hardware architecture, software efficiency, etc. These factors might not affect the throughput in the same proportion, creating disparities between the two. Let's consider cloud deployments that allow for a burst of requests and then reduce the achievable number of requests per second. The reported throughput would not be representative the "common-case" for the entire benchamrk run. 

For these reasons, to have a comprehensive understanding of a system's performance, we need to measure both latency and throughput. The current approach is too optimistic and not representative of the a system's performance over time -- we're doing long running benchmarks and only focusing on the best tini-tiny portion to deduce throughput. **Only by considering both of these metrics can we understand how well a system responds to individual requests and how effectively it processes a large volume of requests over time.**

With the above in mind I would like to propose a PR for this tool that keeps track of throughput from start to end and uses the median/common case value as the reported "Queries per second" value. Agree?
root_cause: Throughput derived from best latency instead of measured across entire benchmark run

issue_title: Feature / Idea: `pip install` for reuse/standardisation
issue_body:
## Overview
[erikbern/ann-benchmarks](https://github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks) is not only an invaluable open-source comparison of popular ANN method (and, I guess, now ANN databases), but it could also provides a solid framework for performance testing/reporting. 

## Motivation
I am looking to build performance testing/benchmarks into [pgvector](https://github.com/pgvector/pgvector) (see [Issue #16](https://github.com/pgvector/pgvector/issues/116)), which currently reports results to ann-benchmarks. It seems superfluous to reinvent the wheel (i.e. dataset process, test execution, metric computation, etc), when pgvector will always want to report to ann-benchmarks too. 

## Scope
The most important functionality for ann-benchmarks to be useful as an import are in `ann_benchmarks/*.py` and the base algorithm implementation [class](https://github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks/blob/main/ann_benchmarks/algorithms/base.py#L6). 
```shell
pip install ann-benchmarks

# For algorithms, all or specific
pip install ann-benchmarks[algorithms]
pip install ann-benchmarks[annoy]

# For datasets (may be preferable to just download and cache)
pip install ann-benchmarks[DEEP1B]
```
This may work better/well with the code restructuring mentioned in #383 .
root_cause: Missing pip installation path prevents modular reuse of benchmark framework

issue_title: Suggestion: Box Plot Summary Charts
issue_body:
As the number of ANN libraries grow, the qps vs recall curves are getting harder to read.
On top of that, each curve only represents one dataset.
Could there possibly be a graphical representation that includes all the information from all the curves in a single, easy to read plot?

I suggest taking inspiration from [The Computer Language 23.03 Benchmarks Game](https://benchmarksgame-team.pages.debian.net/benchmarksgame/box-plot-summary-charts.html).
![fastest](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/946355/231670534-2870b129-f2ea-4377-8a30-ab27cc9f24f6.svg)

In more detail :
1. Convert each plot into a single score for each algorithms. I suggest taking the area under the curve from recall=.5 to recall=1.
2. For each dataset, divide the score of each algorithm by the largest auc of any algorithm. This makes the scores easy to compare between datasets.
3. Compute the mean normalized auc for each algorithm, and list them in order of high to low. Possibly also including standard deviations as in the attached box plot summary chart.

It should be pretty easy to compute, given the raw data. I don't think that is in the git repository though?
root_cause: Missing box plot visualization feature prevents comprehensive performance summary across multiple datasets

issue_title: relax the timeout setting to 24 hours for better qps-recall performance
issue_body: @erikbern , would you please consider relaxing the timeout setting to 24 hours? We found that for some datasets, some algorithms (such as NGTqg and qsgNGT) cannot finish the index building stage within 2 hours, but when the timeout is set to 24 hours, they dould get very good qps-recall performance. Of course, these algorithms’ disadvantage in building time will be reflected in the Recall-build time performance. 24 hours of construction time is indeed a bit long, but for some offline construction applications, it is acceptable to trade construction time for qps-recall performance.
root_cause: Timeout configuration too restrictive for non-parametric algorithm index building

issue_title: Can we use ann-benchmarks to benchmark the All-Nearest-Neighbors problem?
issue_body:
The All-Nearest-Neighbors problem (All-NN) asks us to find all pairs of nearest neighbors in a set of n points in d-dimensions. Classically, there is a [deterministic O(n log n) algorithm](https://link.springer.com/content/pdf/10.1007/BF02187718.pdf), as well as [stochastic O(n sqrt(n))](http://sharif.edu/~zarrabi/papers/cccg-19/ann.pdf) algorithm.

I would like to test the All-NN performance of algorithms like Faiss. According to a google insider, the closed version of faiss does have a mode that solve All-NN. However, I don't know if the public version does.

I suspect it may, and I suspect that ann-benchmarks has a mode that can effectively test All-NN performance via its batch mode. However I don't understand the terminology well enough to determine of batch mode is equivalent to solving All-NN.  Can it?
root_cause: Benchmark dataset preparation lacks All-Nearest-Neighbors test case generation capability

issue_title: category filter
issue_body:
Would it be in the spirit of this benchmarks to add a second benchmark category for ANN in conjunction with categorial filters?

Most real-world applications of ANN will required category filtering e.g. when searching for cloths in an e-commerce scenario via ANN one might filter by gender (categorial) or availability (categorial).

There are several software products which allow combining ANN and category filters e.g. Apache Solr, Elasticsearch, Vertex AI Matching Engine, weaviate, qdrant. However, they mainly differ to this benchmarks in that they are managed services or just services but not embeddable libraries.

In addition to recall vs. queries per second there should be a view which filters to a fraction of the date vs. recall vs. queries per second. For the proprietary managed services, also a cost dimension might be useful.

I have found the following blog articles covering the topic:
* https://blog.vasnetsov.com/posts/categorical-hnsw/
* https://towardsdatascience.com/effects-of-filtered-hnsw-searches-on-recall-and-latency-434becf8041c
* https://towardsdatascience.com/using-approximate-nearest-neighbor-search-in-real-world-applications-a75c351445d

Given that this would be very useful for practical implementations but also the fact that it significantly complicates the benchmarks I would be interested in your opinion and/or how I could help with it. Also I would be great to know if someone has already done such benchmarks.
root_cause: Benchmark lacks synthetic categorical filter generation for realistic ANN evaluation scenarios

issue_title: Relative error metric
issue_body:
I was wondering if there is a way of defining what is a “reasonable” relative error, something similar to recall 0.9, 0.95, 0.99.
Furthermore, how much is this metric is effected by the distance metric?
If you have any experience with this metric I’ll be happy to here about it.
Thanks in advance,
Guy
root_cause: Relative error metric lacks defined thresholds for performance interpretation

issue_title: Wrong x-axis label values (recall) in README
issue_body:
I just went through the ann-benchmarks [paper](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1807.05614.pdf). Now I am wondering whether the values on the x-axis in the README plots should be reversed, going from zero to one and not from one to zero. All figures in the paper (and on ann-benchmarks.com) have x-values going from zero to one with a high number of queries per second when recall is closer to zero.

Shouldn't the recall value be low when there are lots of queries per second and higher when there are less queries per second?

issue_title: Evaluate Elasticsearch approximate kNN API
issue_body:
This was recently released in Elasticsearch 8.0+ and the API is documented [here](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/knn-search-api.html)

Elasticsearch is distinct in both implementation and product from OpenSearch (Amazon maintained fork), and I expect that the two APIs will have different results. Both are HNSW based.

Thanks for maintaining this repo!
root_cause: Missing non-parametric ANN algorithm (Elasticsearch kNN) prevents evaluation of approximate nearest neighbor search

issue_title: spatial search on large data set
issue_body:
I am seeking for pythonic libraries that could be used for spatial searching with good performance using large data set. I am very eager to know if this library can satisfy my needs in terms of execution runtimes and performance.

**_My problem is:_**

I have lots of spheres and want to find corresponding spheres in a distance of each sphere as fast as it is possible because I must call this function many times during my simulation. I have written a python code using numpy and scipy in [one of issues in stack overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/71104627/how-could-i-speed-up-my-written-python-code-spheres-contact-detection-using-spa). But, as it is mentioned in that issue, it is not appropriate for large data set. Is there any prepared example code or any done time-consuming evaluations to investigate capability of this library on this subject? Does this library can perform this spatial searching for 100 nearest spheres on 500000 spheres less than a second or around it? How much time will be your estimation on this subject?

I would appreciate any explanation or for referring to any prepared code to evaluate runtime by this library.

issue_title: Evaluate TorchPQ
issue_body:
Hi,

Thanks for the great work !

I found out TorchPQ recently : https://github.com/DeMoriarty/TorchPQ

> TorchPQ is a python library for Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search (ANNS) and Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS) on GPU using Product Quantization (PQ) algorithm. TorchPQ is implemented mainly with PyTorch, with some extra CUDA kernels to accelerate clustering, indexing and searching.

It could be nice to be added to the list (I'm not the author).

Thanks!
root_cause: GPU support missing from Docker container image setup

issue_title: Support for Jaccard and sparse data
issue_body: I've been working on benchmarking pynndescent on other metrics such as Jaccard, and have been using ``ann-benchmarks`` and the ``kosarak`` dataset for that. Some recent PRs (#235 and #238) have changed things a little, and my initial efforts to get the benchmark working with Kosarak were not successful. After a little experimentation it seems that the data is arriving as a dense boolean 2d array (which is, in itself, easy enough to work with, but a little heavy). I adapted the code the handle this (benefits include getting to use ``pdist`` for the results distance computations), including getting ``nmslib`` up and running with sparse Jaccard (thus ``BallTree``, ``SW-graph`` and ``hnsw`` all work). I was going to put in a PR for this, but then got reading the earlier PRs. Dense boolean arrays was clearly not the intended format from those PRs. Perhaps the ``kosarak`` dataset reference has not been updated on ann-benchmarks.com? Regardless I realised I should seek some clarification about both the current state of the Jaccard benchmark, and the intended data format going forward to ensure I submit something sensible as a PR.
root_cause: Kosarak dataset format mismatch causes dense boolean arrays instead of sparse Jaccard data

issue_title: Format of dataset, queries and output
issue_body:
Hello,
I'm trying to include my own algorithm to benchmark it. However, I'm not sure in which format (type and shape) the dataset, single and batch queries are supplied to the algorithms. In which format should the algorithm return the result? As a list of the indices of the nearest neighbors?
Thanks for your help!
root_cause: Missing documentation on non-parametric algorithm configuration format and interface specification

issue_title: Container exit code is not an integer, but a dictionary
issue_body:
Hej,

Was executing the faiss-ivf and error after what seems to be a succesfull benchmarking run.

> 2021-12-02 15:45:57,143 - annb.0e88ef45e8 - ERROR - Container.wait for container 0e88ef45e8 failed with exception
> Traceback (most recent call last):
>   File "/home/ubuntu/git/ann-benchmarks/ann_benchmarks/runner.py", line 269, in run_docker
>     logger.error('Child process for container %s raised exception %d' % (container.short_id, exit_code))
> TypeError: %d format: a number is required, not dict

I changed the line providing the error message to:
`logger.error(f'Child process for container {container.short_id} raised exception {exit_code}')`

This gives the following output:

> 2021-12-02 15:54:36,931 - annb.a277890f6b - ERROR - Child process for container a277890f6b raised exception {'Error': None, 'StatusCode': 0}

Now, changing the line https://github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks/blob/b95dda65146156ee93c52e0265c8ee5b62bd11dd/ann_benchmarks/runner.py#L265
to: `if exit_code["StatusCode"] != 0:` fixes it.
root_cause: Docker API returns dictionary exit code instead of integer, breaking container execution handling

issue_title: Using custom metrics
issue_body:
Hello,

I wanted to use NN approximation for a problem and I need to use a custom metric for distance calculation between 2 data points. As per my understanding, I would need to implement the algorithm on my own and no library provides such support for adding my own distance score? My problem is related to genomes and the distance between 2 genomes is calculated by an in-house program. Also, to find the best algorithm would require me to test them out rather than only choosing say top 3 because as per my understanding NN search algorithm performance varies with datasets and dimensions. Any help/advice would be appreciated.

issue_title: fail to build diskann
root_cause: DiskANN build fails due to missing AVX-512 CPU instruction set support

issue_title: Running with Parallelism on AWS C5.4x Large Instance Leads to OOM Errors
issue_body: Hey everyone, This is an issue I faced while trying to generate the QPS vs Recall plots for `ann-benchmarks`. The issue is that when building the algorithms with `parallelism = 3`, the available memory gets divided equally between the docker containers running the three algorithms. A typical `AWS C5.4x large` instance comes with 30 GBs of total RAM available. However, when building on top of the ann-benchmarks infrastructure, the maximum RAM availability that I've seen is close to 20 GBs. Hence 20 / 3 = 6.67GBs is the final RAM available while building and running to an algorithm. This leads to an Out-Of-Memory (OOM) error, wherein the `docker` daemon kills the container running the algorithm build if the required memory exceeds the available memory. Hence certain configurations of a heavy algorithm never build to completion, and as such the QPS vs Recall plot is significantly different from the scenario where every algorithm gets to use the full 20 GBs of RAM (in simpler words, everything is run sequentially). For this reason, I believe the QPS vs Recall plots are slightly misleading in their current state as certain build configs wouldn't even run to completion, messing up the final pareto-optimal plot. I believe this also explains why so many dataset plots don't contain results from the full set of algorithms (one example being [this](https://github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks/blob/master/results/gist-960-euclidean.png)). Attaching screenshot for reference. Exit Code 137 refers to OOM killed containers. Command: `sudo python3 run.py --dataset glove-100-angular --parallelism 3`
root_cause: Memory division across parallel containers causes OOM during batch inference execution

issue_title: Question:   The difference between the memory of the container and the index process.
issue_body:
I use the Scann algorithm in the ann-benchmarks framework to run my own data set, the size of the data set is 50M, and the startup command is:
```bash
python3 run.py --algorithm scann --dataset data-50M --batch --timeout 18000
```
Docker container information is:
```
Created container d04dcfa2d5: CPU limit 0-79, mem limit 530102299392, timeout 18000, command ['--dataset', 'data-50M', '--algorithm', 'scann', '--module', 'ann_benchmarks.algorithms.scann', '--constructor', 'Scann', '--runs', '5', '--count', '10', '--batch', '[2000, 0.2, 2, "dot_product"]', '[[1, 30]]', '[[2, 30]]', '[[4, 30]]', '[[8, 30]]', '[[30, 120]]', '[[35, 100]]', '[[40, 80]]', '[[45, 80]]', '[[50, 80]]', '[[55, 95]]', '[[60, 110]]', '[[65, 110]]', '[[75, 110]]', '[[90, 110]]', '[[110, 120]]', '[[130, 150]]', '[[150, 200]]', '[[170, 200]]', '[[200, 300]]', '[[220, 500]]', '[[250, 500]]', '[[310, 300]]', '[[400, 300]]', '[[500, 500]]', '[[800, 1000]]']
```
After the program is finished running, I use grafana to check the container memory. The peak memory is 238G.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/38530078/112310313-654be380-8cdf-11eb-9f00-9fb0d7eeadec.png)

 From the standard output log, the memory used by the indexing process is 54141892.0K ~ 54G. 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/38530078/112309982-fff7f280-8cde-11eb-80f8-b03539cc75c9.png)

Why is there such a big gap?
root_cause: Memory measurement methodology excludes dataset copies and intermediate allocations

issue_title: python3 create_website.py to generate website， ERROR 'Permission denied'
issue_body: Should we execute "chmod -R 777 results" after writing out the result folder every time, otherwise we will not have read permission and we cannot use create_website.py to generate a website？
root_cause: Root-owned result files prevent non-root user from reading cached computations during website generation

issue_title: What does parameter 'verbose' mean?
issue_body: For example, in https://github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks/blob/master/ann_benchmarks/algorithms/faiss_hnsw.py#L18, does it affect search()?

issue_title: Different result from the website
issue_body: I run faiss-ivf and hnsw(faiss) on glove-25-angular dataset in batch mode, and then plot the result: ![glove-25-angular-batch](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/45357009/108654274-4a7d2800-7503-11eb-8897-5d9cf76c82d7.png) While the result post on the [interactive website](http://ann-benchmarks.com/glove-25-angular_10_angular.html) like this: HNSW result seems reasonable, but the trend of faiss-ivf are totally different, is that normal?
root_cause: Batch mode execution produces different performance metrics than website results

issue_title: ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'ann_benchmarks.runner'
issue_body:
Run default experiment Failed.  

# replay method

- Clone the repo.
- Run pip install -r requirements.txt.
- Run python install.py to build all the libraries inside Docker containers (this can take a while, like 10-30 minutes).
- Run python run.py (this can take an extremely long time, potentially days)

issue_title: Downloading datasets is slow
issue_body: The speed of download datasets is slower than 100kB/s, is that the normal speed of your website? Or there is something wrong with my network?

issue_title: Graphs hard to parse for color blind people
issue_body:
Hey very cool project! I find the performance graphs really hard to understand though because of my red-green color blindness. I can imagine many others with worse color blindness having even worse issues. Is there a textual comparison table available? This would help a lot.

Another option could be to make the diagrams interactive and highlight the current selected algorithms performance in some way.
root_cause: Static chart visualization lacks accessibility features and interactive filtering for algorithm comparison

issue_title: Support 'logit' scale for x-axis
issue_body:
The matplotlib `logit` scale is very nice when plotting values between 0 and 1. In particular, this fixes the current problem of plots being incomprehensible in the "high recall" regime.

I suggest changing the `--x-log` and `--y-log` parameters from `plot.py` to `--x-scale` and `--y-scale` taking parameters for plugging directly into `matplotlib`. This will also support the `symlog` scale.

I have a commit with this change here: https://github.com/thomasahle/ann-benchmarks/commit/3bf07f41715a8cfe64b4c7c7bfdaa46027b2892b
root_cause: Matplotlib plot visualization lacks logit scale for better high-recall regime readability

issue_title: knn-graph construction benchmark
issue_body:
Firstly, thank you very much for creating this benchmark. It is very helpful for navigating this exciting but somewhat overwhelming field.

I am interested in particular in the performance of constructing a knn-graph from data points. I wonder whether this would be an interesting additional benchmark. I believe the results might differ somewhat from the existing results, since:

 1. It would combine query performance and index construction in one measure.
 2. It would put techniques which create such a graph as part of their construction (like NN-descent) at an advantage.

What do you think?
root_cause: Benchmark lacks kNN graph construction test inputs and corresponding metrics

issue_title: Default Colors are a bit hard to see on certain algorithms
issue_body:
The default plot colors are too light to clearly see on a white background (especially for faiss-ivf, hnsw(nmslib). I propose a slight modification to the colors to make it more readable in my opinion. What do you folk think?

Code change to ann-benchmarks/plotting/utils.py:
```
def generate_n_colors(n):
    vs = numpy.linspace(0.3, 0.9, 7)
```

Defaults:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/35026793/100823467-38a16100-348f-11eb-84ae-91452c903b88.png)


Updates:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/35026793/100823520-51aa1200-348f-11eb-9948-71565d21b7db.png)
root_cause: Default plot colors lack contrast against white background reducing readability

issue_title: Consider HTML results output
issue_body:
Several graphing libraries in python (e.g. Plotly, Vega) support outputting to interactive HTML that would make it easier for users to understand the data.

Also, you may want to consider making the output results consistent by displaying but graying out results from algorithms that either failed to return outputs or don't support a given data set. For example, `bruteforce-blas` and `scann` are not available on `sift-128-euclidean`.

Thanks for this tool!
root_cause: Missing interactive HTML visualization capability limits result comprehension

issue_title: Installation fails on Ubuntu 18.04
issue_body: Installing via python3 fails running installation.py, because the request 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/milvus-io/docs/v0.6.0/assets/server_config.yaml'' returns 404. The repo structure of milvus-io/docs has changed, URL should be changed to 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/milvus-io/docs/master/v0.6.0/assets/server_config.yaml'. Possibly that once this is fixed, some other URLs from the same repo might be broken.
root_cause: Broken external dependency URLs cause pip installation failure

issue_title: Is there a manual for providing a new dataset for ann-benchmarks?
issue_body: I have a new dataset which has 1 billion data points, and I want it to be used by ann-benchmarks, what do I need to do?
root_cause: Missing documentation for custom dataset integration workflow

issue_title: extracting bruteforce NN class with proper citation
issue_body:
Hi @erikbern et al,
thanks so much for providing this wonderful venue to see benchmarks of ANN implementations. 

I am wondering if you would support me in extracting the [BruteForceBLAS](https://github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks/blob/a851a311ec8ac2488383fdfb06a185d8bc3e8062/ann_benchmarks/algorithms/bruteforce.py#L33) into a separate package and publish it on PyPI and/or conda? I would keep the license (or choose a compliate one). I would also keep the citation so that you and your collaborator's work are properly cited. Your helping hands are of course much appreciated.

In case you are wondering, what I'd need it for, see [this line of research](https://github.com/clovaai/generative-evaluation-prdc). ann-benchmarks at this point brings too many dependencies such that I would include it directly in my projects/repos.

Check

Comparably there isn't 'False' in the following snippet.

Makes any difference?
root_cause: Missing configuration parameter causes algorithm initialization to use incorrect defaults

issue_title: [Question] Are there any ANN methods for sparse data?
issue_body:
I'm wondering if there exist any ANN method suitable for datasets with high number of dimensions but where the dimensions are sparsely populated.

I can see that the typical suggestion is to do dimensionality reduction, which I might end up doing, but before I go that route I was just wondering whether there are any alternatives.

My specific dataset are multiple sequence aligments in bioinformatics. So my data are 3D (n x L x 21). where n is the number of sequences/data points (typically between 50k-10M), L is the length of the sequences (typically somewhere around 80-500) and 21 are the different types of amino acids that each sequence can have.

So in my case I flatten the data to 2d and get (n x 21L) dimensions. However only 1 out of every 21 entries will be nonzero in this case. I'm wondering whether I can use that to my advantage somehow when computing ANN?

issue_title: Is there a way to specify different algo args based on the dataset?
issue_body:
I've been working on integrating elastiknn: https://github.com/alexklibisz/elastiknn
Getting close to making a PR, but I have a case where the good args for the one dataset are different than the good args for another, both with euclidean distance.
I guess I could just include them all, but that seems like a waste of compute resources since I know what's good for one dataset will work poorly for another.
Since ES is on the JVM, data is on disk, and every query is an HTTP request, it's quite a bit slower than the other in-memory C/C++ implementations.
Would hate to bottleneck your updates.

Here's a sneak peek for SIFT (using my own benchmarking plots):
![bokeh_plot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8015228/92657993-cb34c880-f2c3-11ea-8dc0-c20f496299da.png)

 So, is there some way I can setup `algos.yaml` to only use one set of args for dataset A and use another set of args for dataset B?
root_cause: Algorithm parameter configuration lacks dataset-specific stratification capability

issue_title: Is it possible to plot recall vs. latency?
issue_body:
I want to specify latency as x-axis and recall as y-axis when `python plot.py --x-axis --y-axis`.

But I checked metrics.py, there are no metric of latency.
https://github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks/blob/af634f77d0f84a0f377ca079a2197dfe80d90a91/ann_benchmarks/plotting/metrics.py#L100-L152

Anybody has any advice?
root_cause: Missing latency metric in metrics.py prevents chart generation for latency visualization

issue_title: Question: Why ann-benchmarks use distance to compute recall rather than id ?
issue_body: I noticed that the procedure compute the distance between result vectors and query vector, and use these distances to compute recall value. However, the query results could contain vectors id, which can also be used to compute recall value. Why ann-benchmarks chose the former way?

issue_title: Elasticsearch
issue_body: Would be interesting to add: https://opendistro.github.io/for-elasticsearch/features/knn.html
root_cause: Non-parametric ANN algorithm (Elasticsearch OpenDistro KNN) requires benchmark integration

issue_title: ScaNN-only rerun?
issue_body: The ScaNN results currently shown on the website reflect configurations that were only tuned for Glove-100; its performance on Glove-25 and LastFM aren't really reflective of its true, properly tuned performance. I later tuned for these datasets in #172. Would it be possible to update ann-benchmarks.com to reflect these new tunings? I regret not taking more time in polishing the original submission, and I understand you plan on doing a full re-run in a few months, but in the mean time we'd prefer our Glove-25 and LastFM numbers to be more representative of our performance. I don't want every minor tweak to trigger a re-run, but 2-4x more QPS at the same recall is quite significant, and ScaNN trains quite fast so the whole thing should be doable in an hour.
root_cause: Suboptimal hyperparameter tuning causes ScaNN performance underestimation on multiple datasets

issue_title: Benchmark of libnabo
issue_body:
Hi,
Thank you for sharing this really cool work with the community.

I was wondering if you had plans of benchmarking [libnabo](https://github.com/ethz-asl/libnabo). This KNN library is widely used for associating data features for Simultaneous Localization And Mapping (SLAM) by many developers, and it's mentioned in papers about visual localization with inverted search indices by Google.

It would be super useful to gather performance insights as the ones you have extracted from other algorithms.

Best,
Yoshua Nava
root_cause: Non-parametric KNN algorithm (libnabo) missing from benchmark SUT configuration

issue_title: Faiss-lsh
issue_body: We've gotten faiss-ivf working on a really large dataset (54 million vecs). Is there a reason faiss-lsh is disabled in algos.yaml? Should we not even bother using it?

Just wanted to ask a quick question about the Deep1b data set. issue_title: performance on low dimensional datasets
issue_body:
I have tried this algorithm and flann on low dimensional dataset(3-d point cloud).
Here are 800000+ map points, and 300 query points, and find the query points' k neighbor with this algorithm and flann.
the setup of hnswlib is ef_construction = 40, M = 6.
neighbor number k = 10
the performance of two algorithm as follows:
index build time : hnswlib 2249.92 ms flann 207.523 ms
search time : hnswlib 1.3619 ms flann 0.5652 ms
and there are some difference between the search results of these two algorithm, the performance of hnswlib is worse than flann.
Is this reasonable? I'm looking forward to some advice.
here are my dataset and search result.
[dataset.tar.gz](https://github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks/files/4385743/dataset.tar.gz)
[flann_result.txt](https://github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks/files/4385744/flann_result.txt)
[hnswlib_result.txt](https://github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks/files/4385745/hnswlib_result.txt)
root_cause: Non-parametric algorithm (kd-tree) configuration missing for low-dimensional dataset evaluation

issue_title: Sklearn pairwise?
issue_body:
Forgive me for my ignorance, but for a low throughput scenario (i.e. ~10-20 items per second) we used [pairwise cosine](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.metrics.pairwise.cosine_similarity.html) from sklearn which does not suffer from problems that approximate search libraries have (i.e. rebuilding index, adding to index, sharing the index to multiple nodes, etc etc).  

With this you can just minimize your vectors, maybe lower precision and it works decently. The major limitation - you cannot really go beyond 10k - 100k items in the database without search becoming unreasonably slow. The major advantage - you can just store your database as serialized numpy array and add items to it easily.

So my question is, this method was not included because of limited scaling / throughput? Combined with domain heuristics (i.e. you can a priori split your database into 10 chunks) this is a very practical solution.

issue_title: Clone the latest version of NGT
issue_body: [NGT](https://github.com/yahoojapan/NGT) was updated to improve the search performance especially for glove-25. When you run your benchmarks, please clone the latest version (v1.9.0) of NGT.
root_cause: Cached Git repository prevents pulling latest NGT version updates

issue_title: Consider adding a sentence embedding dataset
issue_body:
In addition to word embedding models like GloVe, there are now text embedding models like BERT and Universal Sentence Encoder that work at the level of sentences. These embedding models take an entire sentence as input and output a single vector representation. Sentence embedding models have started to be used alongside kNN to power text search applications.

It would be great to have a dataset based on a sentence embedding model. A sentence vector dataset can show different properties from the set of GloVe word vectors, and kNN algorithms may show a difference in performance between the two.

As an example, a sentence vector dataset could be created by gathering a set of short questions from [Stack Exchange](https://archive.org/details/stackexchange), then running each question through an embedding model like [Universal Sentence Encoder](https://tfhub.dev/google/universal-sentence-encoder).
root_cause: Missing sentence embedding dataset limits benchmark coverage for text search evaluation

issue_title: Consider adding a 'deep image descriptor' dataset
issue_body:
In the past few years, it seems the computer vision community has expanded out from SIFT descriptors and started working with 'deep descriptors' of images from convolutional neural nets. It would be helpful to include a dataset of deep image descriptors to more closely represent this approach to image similarity search.

One idea is to take a sample from [this large dataset](http://sites.skoltech.ru/compvision/noimi/), which consists of images from ImageNet run through the a CNN to produce vectors. Some prominent kNN papers report results against this dataset, including [Billion-scale similarity search with GPUs](https://arxiv.org/abs/1702.08734) and [Efficient and robust approximate nearest neighbor search using Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/1603.09320).
root_cause: Missing deep image descriptor dataset limits benchmark representation of modern vision methods

issue_title: Batch queries and single-threaded mode

There are currently two relicts in the code which are not accessible since the arguments for them have been removed. What shall we do with them? 

1) Running an algorithm with `runner.py` has a `force_single` which avoids the thread-pooling mechanism altogether, independent of the `use_threads` method of an algorithm. I find it nice for checking whether the ThreadPool decreases performance. 

2) Running an algorithm also has support for batch mode and each algorithm already implements `batch_query`. However, only `faiss_gpu.py` currently implements an actual batch mode. However, HNSW and faiss implementations actually have good batch query implementations and they can easily be included to the benchmark in a different section.
root_cause: Batch query and single-threaded execution modes removed from CLI arguments, blocking benchmark execution paths

issue_title: 10X performance difference between multi-thread and single thread tests
issue_body:
Good day!

I have measured the difference in performance when switching the use_threads parameter from True to False on hnsw (nmslib). As it turns out, the difference is really huge, ~ 10X on 1M SIFT for low recalls (see enclosed plots for a 6-core intel cpu), and likely is caused by the python-part overhead of the tester scripts.

Multi-thread (default):
![sift_mt](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/16265186/34437153-f7afe4fa-ecac-11e7-8f1c-cf382ef9a4b2.png)
Single-thread (use_threads=False):
![sift_st](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/16265186/34437154-f7cada12-ecac-11e7-9109-a8377da007d1.png)

This makes the current results questionable, especially when compared to FALCONN (which seems to be the only algorithm which takes advantage of not using multi-thread tests). Multithread tests also significantly change the shape of the recall-QPS curve (flat vs rapidly decreasing QPS at low recall).

I suggest using the single thread version for all algorithms. Otherwise, for the case of relatively high QPS (~>2 kps) we just measure the performance of the tester script.
root_cause: Python thread pool overhead contaminates performance measurements during batch inference

issue_title: Split up building and query parameters
issue_body:
Currently, we spend a lot of time basically building the same data structure over and over again. What we could do instead, is split up _build_ and _query_ parameters. For example, Annoy is currently configured by

```
    annoy:
      constructor: Annoy
      base-args: ["@metric"]
      run-groups:
        annoy:
          args: [[100, 200, 400], [100, 200, 400, 1000, 2000, 4000, 10000,
                20000, 40000, 100000, 200000, 400000]] # number of trees x number of points to gather
```

It would save a lot of time, if one were to separate query and build parameters in the following way: 
```
    annoy:
      constructor: Annoy
      base-args: ["@metric"]
      run-groups:
        annoy:
          build-args: [[100, 200, 400]] # number of trees 
          query-args: [[100, 200, 400, 1000, 2000, 4000, 10000,
                20000, 40000, 100000, 200000, 400000]] # number of points to gather during query
```
The idea would now be to build the data structure for each `build-args` group, and query it for all (combinations of) `query-args`. This should be rather straight-forward to do.

Should be labeled as "enhancement".
root_cause: Inefficient benchmark configuration causes repeated index building during evaluation

issue_title: index construction time plotting
issue_body:
I plotted index construction time using data saved in glove.txt and see a spread of almost 2 orders of magnitude (hnsw(nmslib) index construction might have been parallelized according to code).  I'm most interested in the performance of sw-graph vs hnsw.  It's very hard to say which one is better because one might be able to improve the search performance of sw-graph by building more aggressive indices.  I collaborated with Princeton folks on developing a hierarchical version of the algorithm behind KGraph and the results were negative.  Initially there was noticeable speedup at the same accuracy, but after carefully controlling index construction setup, the speedup was gone.

I also suggest that we establish a standard hardware configuration to run the benchmark.  Preferrably some type of AWS instance which can be easily reproduced.  I think something close to your current setup should be good.

![build](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/5427210/15939002/da24341e-2e43-11e6-9dbc-3944177274e7.png)
![throughput](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/5427210/15939003/da294b5c-2e43-11e6-940a-2b0b70c000a6.png)
root_cause: Index construction time visualization lacks standardized hardware configuration for reproducible benchmarking

issue_title: float32 vs float64
issue_body:
It appears that the benchmark has switched from float32 to float64 quite a while ago, and this somehow contributed to a substantial performance regression of KGraph, which is only optimized for float32 although float64 also runs.

Below are my justifications for using float32 in the context of approximate K-NN search:
- The search process is usually approximate, and the feature data are usually noisy by nature, so typically the precision of float64 is not necessary.
- The necessity of an index to speed up search presumes that data is big.  So a factor of 2x in the cost just to store the data in memory is significant.
- For some algorithm/implementation, using float32 means more data can be fit into cache and the code being much faster.

I believe the performance behavior of some library will be very different for float32 and float64 (though I believe the relative ranking of different libraries should roughly stay the same).  If only one can be chosen, I feel it should be float32 so that the benchmark represents the typical use case.  

Is there a particular reason to keep float64, or it's better supported by some libraries in the benchmark?
(There's another reason why KGraph is slow which I'm still investigating.)
root_cause: Benchmark data type configuration (float32 vs float64) affects SUT performance measurement

issue_title: String "None" defaults cause TypeError in UES/IDP evaluation
issue_body:
Title: TypeError: string indices must be integers when using default OpenAI path in UES/IDP

Description:

When running UES/IDP evaluation with the standard OpenAI API (not Azure, not vLLM), the evaluation fails with "TypeError: string indices must be integers, not 'str'" if azure_openai_config is not explicitly provided in the config.

Root Cause:

In ares/ares.py, several optional parameters use the string "None" as the default value instead of Python None:

    "documents": (int, "None"),           # Line 32
    "azure_openai_config": (dict, "None"), # Line 36  
    "host_url": (str, "None")              # Line 37

Since the string "None" is truthy, the condition "if azure_openai_config:" in ues_idp.py evaluates to True, causing the code to incorrectly enter the Azure OpenAI branch. The Azure branch then attempts dictionary access on the string "None", which raises the TypeError.

Steps to Reproduce:

    from ares import ARES

    config = {
        'in_domain_prompts_dataset': 'few_shot.tsv',
        'unlabeled_evaluation_set': 'unlabeled.tsv',
        'model_choice': 'gpt-3.5-turbo-0125',
        'documents': 1,
    }

    ares = ARES(ues_idp=config)
    results = ares.ues_idp()  # Raises TypeError

Error Output:

    TypeError: string indices must be integers, not 'str'

Suggested Fix:

Replace string "None" defaults with falsy values that work correctly with truthiness checks:

    "documents": (int, 0),
    "azure_openai_config": (dict, {}),
    "host_url": (str, "")

And update the corresponding checks in ues_idp.py from string comparisons to truthiness checks.

Environment:

    ARES version: 0.6.6
    Python: 3.11
    OpenAI SDK: 2.14.0

Workaround:

Explicitly pass azure_openai_config=None in the config dictionary.
root_cause: String 'None' default causes azure_openai_config truthiness check to incorrectly enter Azure branch

issue_title: Errors in tutorials and missing module for PPI evaluation
issue_body:
Hello,

I encountered issues in each of the tutorials, requiring modifications to the code to make them work.

Now, during the evaluation with PPI, I get the following error:
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'ares.LLM_as_a_Judge_Adaptation.Late_Chunking_Classifier'

This indicates that the module "Late_Chunking_Classifier" does not exist, which is clearly not normal.
Additionally, just before encountering this error, I had to manually add the parameter "use_late_chunking", which seems to be required but is not defined anywhere in the code!

Could you please provide clarification or a fix for these issues?

Thank you!
root_cause: Missing Late_Chunking_Classifier module breaks judge model preparation

issue_title: Low-quality synthetic questions are not actually filtered
issue_body: filter_synthetic_queries does not actually filter questions, but simply changes their label, however in generate_synthetic_queries these labels are changed back to "Yes"
root_cause: Label mutation logic breaks synthetic data filtering pipeline integrity

issue_title: What is meant by contrastive learning objective?
issue_body:
Having read the paper and the code I don't understand what is meant by "These judges are lightweight models fine-tuned against a contrastive learning objective.". 

The loss function is a cross entropy loss and the task is a classification task (yes/no) for belonging or not belonging in a class. There is no notion in the code of maximizing the separation of these classes in the embeddings space. The phrasing adds some confusion since contrastive typically means something else.

issue_title: No strong negative generation
issue_body: I was wondering about the strong negative generation. This is mentioned in the paper but does not actually seem te be used in the code. Could you elaborate?

issue_title: Difference between Answer Relevance and Answer Faithfulness
issue_body:
Hello,

I was wondering whether there is any difference between Answer Relevance and Answer Faithfulness. Conceptually there is of course, but the code for training LLM judges and actually judging samples seems exactly the same. Would it not make sense for the answer relevance metric to not take into account the context during training and judging?
root_cause: Judge preparation implementation treats Answer Faithfulness and Answer Relevance identically despite conceptual differences

issue_title: Source of nq_labeled_output.tsv
issue_body:
Hello,

The nq_labeled_output.tsv file contains 6189 rows from NQ. As I understand this file was used to generate the synthetic data to train the LLM Judges. Where was this file sourced? Specifically, which split from NQ is this from and how where the rows selected. I am trying to understand how one would apply ARES to a new dataset (and evaluate it) i.e. creating synthetic data, training the judges and evaluating on mock datasets. I assume the documents used in the human reference set and the mock splits should be separate from the ones used to generate the synthetic data. I was also wondering how the provided synthetic data was generated. I see the file for NQ has precisely 3000 rows. Were all 6189 documents used like in the examples and was the set shrunk later?
root_cause: Judge preparation data sourcing and generation methodology undocumented

issue_title: Error while evaluating with PPI with API model LLM judge
issue_body:
I am using ares evaluator with the given data in the docs to evaluate using prediction powered inference
```yaml
ppi_config {
        evaluation_datasets: ['./aresdocs/nq_unlabeled_output_trimmed.tsv']
        few_shot_examples_filepath: "./aresdocs/nq_few_shot_prompt_for_judge_scoring.tsv"
        llm_judge: "gpt-4o-mini"
        labels: ["Context_Relevance_Label",]
        gold_label_paths: ["./aresdocs/nq_labeled_output_trimmed.tsv"]
}
```

Tried models: openai models: [gpt4, gpt-4o-mini], multiple together models
Error:
```bash
 File "python3.10/site-packages/ares/RAG_Automatic_Evaluation/LLMJudge_RAG_Compared_Scoring.py", line 729, in evaluate_model
    if total_references.nelement() > 0:
AttributeError: 'numpy.ndarray' object has no attribute 'nelement'
```

If I use default model provided checkpoint with config, It works successfully.

```yaml
}
```

Do I need to provide the checkpoints every time ?

issue_title: Reproducing results
issue_body:
Hello,

I am trying to reproduce the results in the examples, and eventually the paper. However, when running the PPI step from the Collab notebook I get curious results.

I run the following:
```
from ares import ARES

ppi_config = {
    "evaluation_datasets": ["datasets/example_files/nq_unlabeled_output.tsv"],
    "checkpoints": ["Context_Relevance_Label_joint_trained_date_time.pt", "Answer_Relevance_Label_joint_trained_date_time.pt"],
    "labels": ["Context_Relevance_Label", "Answer_Relevance_Label"],
    "gold_label_paths": ["datasets/example_files/nq_labeled_output.tsv"],
}

ares_module = ARES(ppi=ppi_config)
results = ares_module.evaluate_RAG()
print(results)
```

However, for context relevance I get this:
```
Context_Relevance_Label Scoring
ARES Ranking
Evaluation_Set:datasets/example_files/nq_unlabeled_output.tsv
Checkpoint:Context_Relevance_Label_joint_trained_date_time.pt
ARES Prediction: [0.595499509914802]
ARES Confidence Interval: [[0.541, 0.65]]
Number of Examples in Evaluation Set: [4421]
Ground Truth Performance: [0.6]
ARES LLM Judge Accuracy on Ground Truth Labels: [0.629]
Annotated Examples used for PPI: 300
```

Which is exactly the numbers for answer relevance in the notebook.

For answer relevance I get:
```
Answer_Relevance_Label Scoring
ARES Ranking
Evaluation_Set:datasets/example_files/nq_unlabeled_output.tsv
Checkpoint:Answer_Relevance_Label_joint_trained_date_time.pt
ARES Prediction: [0.5955191133227766]
ARES Confidence Interval: [[0.577, 0.614]]
Number of Examples in Evaluation Set: [4421]
Ground Truth Performance: [0.6]
ARES LLM Judge Accuracy on Ground Truth Labels: [0.977]
Annotated Examples used for PPI: 300
```

Any idea why this might be happening?

Full results output:

`[[{'Label_Column': 'Context_Relevance_Label', 'Evaluation_Set': 'datasets/example_files/nq_unlabeled_output.tsv', 'ARES_Prediction': 0.595499509914802, 'ARES_Confidence_Interval': [0.541, 0.65], 'Number_of_Examples_in_Evaluation_Set': 4421, 'Ground_Truth_Performance': 0.6, 'ARES_LLM_Judge_Accuracy_on_Ground_Truth_Labels': 0.629, 'Annotated_Examples_used_for_PPI': 300}], [{'Label_Column': 'Answer_Relevance_Label', 'Evaluation_Set': 'datasets/example_files/nq_unlabeled_output.tsv', 'ARES_Prediction': 0.5955191133227766, 'ARES_Confidence_Interval': [0.577, 0.614], 'Number_of_Examples_in_Evaluation_Set': 4421, 'Ground_Truth_Performance': 0.6, 'ARES_LLM_Judge_Accuracy_on_Ground_Truth_Labels': 0.977, 'Annotated_Examples_used_for_PPI': 300}]]`
root_cause: Incorrect context relevance checkpoint causes judge prediction mismatch

issue_title: Unclear about the code of calculate_ppi function
issue_body:
Hi 

It seems unclear what is the reason for creating 20 random values  `ns = np.linspace(0, n_max, 20).astype(int)` to determine the number of labelled data used to calculate the PPI, but in the end only the PPI value calculated from `n_max` is retained as seen from `avg_ci = ci.mean(axis=0)[-1]`

I don't understand what is the purpose of conducting multiple trials? Since only the only the PPI value calculated from `n_max` is retained, it should be constant throughout the trials isn't it?

https://github.com/stanford-futuredata/ARES/blob/2684d477878e515c3dc31cf4b91fb848a84bdb90/ares/RAG_Automatic_Evaluation/LLMJudge_RAG_Compared_Scoring.py#L238-L281

Also, I saw there there were other functions from the original PPI repository (PPI bootstrap, cross PPI etc), what were you consideration when choosing which PPI function to use?
